project_overview.project_development_objective: To reduce the quantity of untreated municipal sewage discharged into the Litani River and to improve pollution management around Qaraoun Lake
project_overview.total_financing: US$60 million (IBRD: US$55 million, GoL: US$5 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Direct beneficiaries: approximately 344,000 people (49% female), including 340,000 people with improved access to sanitation facilities, 750 farmers trained in sustainable practices, and 50+ government staff trained in water management. Indirect beneficiaries: approximately 800,000 people in Beqaa Governorate and 525,000 Syrian refugees in the region.

primary_locations: Beqaa Valley (Zahlé, West Beqaa, Baalbeck, Hermel, Rachaya districts)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - The project is located in the Beqaa region which is affected by political instability, security incidents, and hosts approximately 525,000 Syrian refugees (35% of total Syrian refugees in Lebanon). The region is economically disadvantaged with high poverty rates (highest concentration of poor in the country). Political uncertainty and absence of presidential elections since May 2014 have impacted government priorities and decision-making capacity.

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Improvement of Municipal Sewage Collection
key_components.description: Expansion of sewerage networks to connect to three wastewater treatment plants (Zahlé, Anjar, and Aitanit WWTPs). Includes construction of approximately 200 km of new sewerage network, rehabilitation of existing networks, and approximately 7,300 new household connections. Subcomponent 1.1 (Zahlé): 108 km network, 6,000 connections. Subcomponent 1.2 (Anjar): 135 km network for 10 localities. Subcomponent 1.3 (Aitanit): network expansion for 4 villages. Subcomponent 1.4: Design, supervision, and safeguards consultancy services.
key_components.financing: US$55.5 million (IBRD: US$50.5 million, GoL: US$5 million)

key_components.component_name: Component 2: Promotion of Good Agricultural Practices (including Integrated Pest Management)
key_components.description: Training of approximately 750 large farmers in West Beqaa and Zahlé districts on sustainable agricultural practices to reduce fertilizer and pesticide use. Implementation through Field Schools methodology. Targets reduction of nitrogen fertilizer use by 50% (from 31 kg/dunum to 15 kg/dunum) and pesticide use by 40-50%. Includes baseline surveys, farm visits, and monitoring of agrochemical use.

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Solid Waste Management, Water Quality Monitoring, Capacity Building, and Project Management
key_components.description: Subcomponent 3.1: Technical studies for solid waste management (sorting facility in Rachaya, closure/rehabilitation of 8 dump sites, recruitment of solid waste expert). Subcomponent 3.2: Improvement of water quality monitoring network (expansion from 10 to 20 monitoring locations), water resources modeling, awareness campaigns. Subcomponent 3.3: Capacity building for Beqaa Water Establishment (recruitment of wastewater engineer, customer relations officer, financial officer). Subcomponent 3.4: Capacity building for Ministry of Energy and Water. Subcomponent 3.5: Project management unit establishment and operation.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Council for Development and Reconstruction (CDR) as the primary implementing agency, in close coordination with Ministry of Energy and Water (MoEW), Ministry of Environment (MoE), Ministry of Agriculture (MoA), Litani River Authority (LRA), Beqaa Water Establishment (BWE), and FAO for Component 2
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Management Unit (PMU) established at CDR headed by a Project Coordinator. Technical experts recruited as part of PMU but seconded to respective line ministries/agencies. Qaraoun Committee (established May 2014) provides oversight and ensures coherence with other investments in the Upper Litani watershed. Regular coordination meetings and monthly monitoring by Qaraoun Committee.
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: International Competitive Bidding (ICB) and National Competitive Bidding (NCB) for major works contracts. Quality and Cost-Based Selection (QCBS), Fixed Budget Selection (FBS), Least-Cost Selection (LCS), Consultant Qualifications Selection (CQS), and Single Source Selection (SSS) for consulting services. FAO engaged through single-source selection for Component 2 due to unique technical expertise. Prior review thresholds: US$500,000 for goods, US$5 million for works, US$200,000 for consulting firms, US$100,000 for individual consultants.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Semi-annual progress reports prepared by PMU covering all project indicators. Monitoring of PDO indicators: direct beneficiaries, female beneficiaries percentage, quantity of municipal wastewater collected and treated (daily flow in m³), nutrient load reduction (nitrogen in tons/year), number of water quality monitoring locations. Intermediate results indicators include sewer network constructed (km), household connections, farmers adopting improved technologies, trash removed from river banks, consultation participants. Water quality monitoring by LRA using Water Quality Index (WQI) at 20 locations. External audit of project financial statements annually. Mid-term review in year 3.

identified_risks.risk_description: Political instability and absence of presidential elections since May 2014 have impacted government priorities and decision-making capacity. Ongoing Syrian conflict creates security concerns in the Beqaa region, potentially limiting mobility of consultants and contractors, hindering Bank supervision missions, and reducing government implementation capacity.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strong ownership and commitment demonstrated by MoE and MoEW. MoE has taken all necessary steps for project preparation and consultation. Flexible project approach ready for restructuring if needed. Continuous engagement with Council of Ministers and Parliament to maintain political support. Qaraoun Committee provides institutional coordination mechanism.

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak institutional capacity of Beqaa Water Establishment (BWE) for long-term operation and maintenance of water and wastewater infrastructure. BWE has limited financial and technical capacity, approximately 400 vacant positions unfilled due to government hiring freeze, weak financial management (cash-basis accounting, no proper asset management), and low cost recovery (50% of O&M costs in 2013).
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Dedicated capacity building component (Component 3.3) to strengthen BWE with recruitment of three high-level staff (wastewater engineer, customer relations officer, financial officer). Training and mentoring of BWE staff during implementation. Outsourcing of O&M to private sector where appropriate. Financial projections show potential to reach 90% cost recovery by 2022. Involvement of BWE from project inception in design and implementation.

identified_risks.risk_description: Risk of delays in construction of Anjar WWTP funded through Italian Protocol. Detailed design for subcomponents 1.2 and 1.3 not yet finalized, creating uncertainty about final costs and implementation timeline. Potential for cost overruns and schedule delays.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Planned scheduling of household connections for Anjar in later years of project to allow maximum time for WWTP construction. Project remains economically viable even if Anjar component is excluded (IRR of 15% vs. 17% base case). Sensitivity analysis shows project remains viable with 20% cost increase (IRR of 13%). Close coordination with Italian Protocol implementers. Detailed designs for subcomponents 1.2 and 1.3 to be prepared during implementation before construction starts.

identified_risks.risk_description: CDR has experienced delays in submission of audit reports and lacks proper maintenance of asset lists. Contract management capacity is weak, resulting in delayed decision-making and contract amendments.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of qualified procurement specialist and contract management consultants. Operationalization of assets module in CDR accounting software. Engagement of acceptable external auditor in early stages of project. Use of Bank's standard bidding documents and RFP templates. Semi-annual procurement supervision missions. Capacity building support to CDR FM team.

identified_risks.risk_description: Expansion of sewerage networks involves civil works with potential environmental impacts. Land acquisition required for pipeline routes (estimated 6,221 m² for Zahlé component). Potential minor land acquisition and temporary disruptions during construction.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental Assessment (Category B) with site-specific EIA/EMP prepared for Zahlé WWTP connection. Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) prepared for subcomponents 1.2 and 1.3 with site-specific EIA/EMP to be prepared before construction. Resettlement Policy Framework prepared following OP 4.12. Land acquisition plan prepared and disclosed. Grievance redress mechanism established with multi-level procedures. Consultation with government and public stakeholders conducted. Environmental specialist included in PMU. Integrated Pest Management component (Component 2) will reduce pesticide and fertilizer use, providing environmental benefits.

identified_risks.risk_description: Lebanon's economy faces challenges with political uncertainty, regional conflicts, and refugee crisis affecting economic growth. GDP growth declined from 8% in 2010 to 1.5% in 2015. Approximately 1.5 million Lebanese and 1.5 million Syrian refugees live below poverty line.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project targets one of the poorest regions (Beqaa) with high concentration of vulnerable populations. Improved water and sanitation services will provide direct benefits to poor communities. Project contributes to poverty reduction and improved public health. Tariff policy recommendations include progressive block tariff structure to protect low-income households.

identified_risks.risk_description: Implementation of National Wastewater Strategy requires coordination across multiple agencies and sustained commitment to tariff increases and cost recovery. Weak enforcement of environmental regulations and penalties for water pollution.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project aligned with National Wastewater Strategy (2012) and National Water Sector Strategy. Qaraoun Committee provides coordination mechanism for multi-agency implementation. Capacity building for MoEW (Component 3.4) to oversee sector performance and monitor strategy implementation. Tariff policy recommendations developed. Project supports implementation of Business Plan for Combating Pollution in Qaraoun Lake.

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential resistance from communities regarding land acquisition, construction disruptions, and tariff increases. Low collection rates (35% in 2013) indicate customer reluctance to pay for water services. Approximately 30-50% of households illegally connected or not paying bills.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Multi-level grievance redress mechanism established. Consultation with government and public stakeholders conducted during preparation. Awareness campaigns planned (Component 3.2) to raise awareness about water quality and need for payment. Customer relations officer to be recruited (Component 3.3) to improve engagement. Outreach activities to improve fee collection in underperforming areas. Progressive tariff structure to protect low-income households. Community involvement in trash removal campaigns.

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Multi-level grievance redress mechanism established: (1) Affected persons file written grievance with concerned municipalities within 14 days; (2) If unresolved, grievance escalated to CDR Expropriation Unit within 14 days; (3) Unresolved grievances submitted to Appeals Committee with final decision within 3 months; (4) Final recourse to Courts of Law according to Lebanese law. Also eligible for World Bank's institutional grievance redress mechanism and Inspection Panel.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Project targets 49% female beneficiaries among direct beneficiaries. Women farmers included in training on good agricultural practices and integrated pest management. Field Schools methodology includes women farmers. Gender-disaggregated monitoring of beneficiaries and farmers adopting improved technologies.
vulnerable_groups: Approximately 1.5 million Lebanese living below poverty line (28% of population)
Approximately 1.5 million Syrian refugees (30% of Lebanon's population, 35% concentrated in Beqaa region)
Low-income households in Beqaa region (highest poverty concentration in country)
Rural populations with limited access to sanitation services
Farmers dependent on agriculture in Beqaa Valley

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project is designed to address environmental degradation and improve public health in a fragile context. Improved water and sanitation services will benefit vulnerable populations including Syrian refugees. Project supports regional stability by addressing environmental issues that could exacerbate tensions. Qaraoun Committee provides coordination mechanism for multi-stakeholder engagement. Project avoids exacerbating conflicts by ensuring inclusive consultation and grievance mechanisms. Awareness campaigns and community engagement activities promote social cohesion.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Lebanon faces significant fragility factors: (1) Political instability with no presidential elections since May 2014, impacting government decision-making; (2) Ongoing Syrian conflict with 1.5 million Syrian refugees (30% of population) creating strain on services and economy; (3) High poverty rates with 28% of population living below poverty line, concentrated in Beqaa region; (4) Weak institutional capacity, particularly in water sector; (5) Security incidents in Beqaa region affecting project implementation; (6) Economic challenges with GDP growth declining from 8% (2010) to 1.5% (2015); (7) Limited government capacity to enforce environmental regulations. Project targets one of the most fragile regions with highest poverty concentration and largest refugee population.
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Beqaa region experiences security incidents related to Syrian conflict. Project implementation may be hindered by limited mobility of consultants and contractors, difficulty in conducting Bank supervision missions, and reduced government implementation capacity. Flexible project design allows for restructuring if security situation deteriorates. Project team maintains flexible approach and is prepared to adapt implementation arrangements. Security clearances may be required for international staff. Local recruitment prioritized where possible.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Project area hosts approximately 525,000 Syrian refugees (35% of total Syrian refugees in Lebanon), concentrated in Beqaa region. Project will provide indirect benefits to refugee populations through improved water quality and sanitation services. Refugee population contributes to increased solid waste generation (estimated 37.7% additional waste from Syrian refugees). Project includes solid waste management component to address increased waste from refugee populations. No direct displacement expected, though minor land acquisition required for pipeline routes (6,221 m² for Zahlé component). Resettlement Policy Framework prepared to address any land acquisition issues. Grievance redress mechanism available for affected persons including refugees.
